Patch Set 6: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

let's postpone this whole pin/unpin thing till the later phases... I don't think it's reasonable to put this very specific and confusing code in the core. it doesn't look like to be re-used by anybody else.

Patch Set 6:

What's wrong with it? It just hold one reference count when pin(), and put such reference count when unpin(). The unpin() can be triggered by the system purge thread if there are memory pressure.

I think it is just what you described in former 4905 comment.

Any others who want to use pin()/unpin() can follow the same mechanism, why cannot be shared?

Patch Set 6:

> What's wrong with it? It just hold one reference count when pin(), and put such reference count when unpin(). The unpin() can be triggered by the system purge thread if there are memory pressure.
I think it is just what you described in former 4905 comment.

I told few times I'd prefer do not put very specific code into the core. I consider this as a very specific code solving very specific problem. which adds extra 16 bytes per object to be used very rare.

> Any others who want to use pin()/unpin() can follow the same mechanism, why cannot be shared?

who else? I barely can imagine any at the moment.

Patch Set 6:

Then how can we implement the functionality pin()/unpin() without changing the object data structure? Or can we reuse lu_object_header::loh_lru, but that means we need to change core logic for get()/put() operations. I am not sure whether it is your want or not.

Patch Set 6:

the easy way is just to get a hash/list of the pointers to the objects in LFSCK.

Patch Set 6:

Compared with my current patch, Hash pointers to pinned objects is not "the easy way". My current patch consumes more bytes as you pointing out, but on some how, it exchanges between space and time. If it is really unnecessary, can we consider to optimize it in LFSCK phase IV?

Patch Set 6:

I'd prefer do not put anything related to pin/unpin in the current patch then.

we can discuss this later..  I'd like to see this mechanism:
1) internal to LFSCK
2) do not introducing overhead to the core lu mechanism

Patch Set 6:

remember that most of time that extra bytes are wasted.. take into account how often LFSCK will be running and finding files with nlink>1.

Patch Set 6:

Can we use the following mechanism temporary:

1) hold additional reference on the pinned object.

2) do not allow the pinned object to be purged, even if there are memory pressure.

Then we can just process it internal LFSCK without changing lu core mechanism.

Since multiple-linked objects are rare, the RAM consumed by the pinned objects should be very limited. And even if there is memory pressure, releasing the RAM occupied by the pinned object cannot help much.

How do you think?

Patch Set 6:

I don't think it makes sense to introduce now some mechanism which is likely to  be changed *soon*. I'm not against pinning some limited number of objects, but again - why do not just do this later?

Patch Set 6:

If we do not implement pin()/unpin(), then the verification for multiple-linked objects much be inefficient, because they will be released as the LFSCK processing. We have mentioned that in our design document already.

I am not sure whether it is acceptable or not if we do not implement the promised functionality.

Anyway, if you and Andreas agree with it, we can put the performance issues aside and consider to resolve them in LFSCK phase IV.

Patch Set 6:

they won't be released immediately, they are subject to regular cache purge.

Patch Set 6:

They are not release immediately, but they are will be used when double scan, at that time, all the objects in the system have been scanned at least once, so it is quite possible that the (to be pinned) objects have been released already.
Uploaded patch set 7.

http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/11872/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 7:

Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 7:

if I understand the design correctly they will be used again if verification in the first pass wasn't successful. which is supposed to be rare as well. IOW, the chance to meet such a case and need to seek is very very rare.. in the normal cases the first pass is supposed to find all the links and remove the object from that special index, right ?

Patch Set 7:

For a given object, if it has crashed linkEA entry, even though it is not multiple-linked object, before the first cycle scanning finished, we cannot know whether such linkEA entry is valid or not. So we have to record such object and better the verification history. Then during the double scan against such recorded object, we can easy to find out the crashed linkEA entry.

Anyway, the inconsistent cases should be rare.

parents.subject: LU-1866 lfsck: general framework for LFSCK 1.5

commit.subject: LU-1866 lfsck: LFSCK main engine
commit.message:
LU-1866 lfsck: LFSCK main engine

There are two engines for LFSCK 1.5:

1) One is low layer object table based iteration, which can
   guarantee that all the objects can be scanned at least once.

2) The other is namespace based directory traversal. All the
   namespace based components, such as FID-in-dirent and linkEA,
   can be handled during that.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: Ic5a404cde35fdb20c24f6522faa8b21117cf4254
